module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The partner of a patient on the intensive care has asked to see you. The patient is a 67-year-old woman admitted with a community acquired pneumonia caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae. She was previously fit and well. She has developed a stage 2 AKI in the first 48 hours of admission and the partner is very worried about this development and has asked to speak to a doctor. The partner wants to understand how serious the AKI is and asks what the short- and long-term issues are. You tell her:
options: A: AKI stage 2 is extremely serious and decreases the patient's chances of survival from her community acquired pneumonia. She is likely to require renal replacement therapy and her risk of developing chronic kidney disease are markedly increased.

B: AKI stage 2 is not too serious and is most likely to recover. If it doesn't get better over the first week, it is considered an acute kidney disease but even then, it would be expected to get better without any serious consequences.

C: AKI stage 2 is serious and is associated with her severity of illness and risk of poor outcome. However, AKI and CKD are very different diseases and AKI is not associated with long term risk of CKD so long as it recovers quickly.

D: The development of an AKI is serious and is associated with her severity of illness and risk of poor outcome. You would hope that her AKI recovers quickly, but it could get worse requiring renal replacement therapy and if persistent could develop into a more established AKD and later CKD. She is at increased risk of renal decline in the future.
questions.explanation: All AKI, regardless of severity and stage, is associated with a poorer prognosis from critical illness. An AKI lasting more than 7 days is now classified as AKD and all AKI is associated with an increased risk of CKD in the future. Option A overstates the immediate severity. Option B underestimates the seriousness. Option C incorrectly states AKI is not associated with long-term CKD risk.
questions.topic: AKI prognosis AKD CKD risk communication
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The following statements are true regarding renal recovery after AKI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Patients who have a recurrence of AKI during their hospital admission have an increased risk of mortality at 1 year.

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Patients who develop AKD during their admission have no long-term sequelae if their renal function returns to baseline prior to discharge.

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Patients who develop AKI and recover within 24–48 hours remain at increased risk of renal morbidity.

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. The development of both AKI and AKD, regardless of initial recovery is associated with increased risk of CKD.

questions.explanation: Recurrent AKI before discharge is associated with significantly increased 1-year mortality. Patients with AKI who recover within 1 week have improved outcome compared with AKD, but even recovered AKI carries increased risk of CKD progression and 1-year mortality. All episodes of AKI are associated with increased risk of CKD — patients who recover quickly are most likely to do well but risk of relapse and progression may persist. AKD patients remain at increased CKD risk even if renal function returns to apparent baseline.
questions.topic: AKI recovery AKD recurrence CKD prognosis mortality
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question:
A 46-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity (BMI 38), ischaemic heart disease, a pre-operative haemoglobin of 105 g/L and CKD stage 2, is admitted still intubated and ventilated to the general ICU after a difficult anterior resection for a rectal carcinoma. He required 3 units of blood during surgery and is admitted to the unit on 0.4 µg/kg/min of noradrenaline. His capillary refill time is > 3 seconds and he is cold with a temperature of 35.2 °C. His blood pressure is 110/55 mmHg, heart rate is a regular 126 beats per minute. His arterial blood gas reveals adequate oxygenation on FiO2 of 0.4, a pH 7.27, PaCO2 4.3 kPa (32 mmHg), blood glucose of 14.9 mmol/L, base excess of -6.8 and lactate of 4.1 mmol/L. His urine output is 15 mL/h and haemoglobin is 71 g/L.

The modifiable risk factors in the ICU for developing an AKI include:
options: A: Hypovolaemia, anaemia, hyperglycaemia
B: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, anaemia, malignancy, hypovolaemia, CKD stage 2
C: Hyperglycaemia, anaemia, malignancy, obesity
D: Malignancy, ischaemic heart disease, obesity
questions.explanation: This patient has several risk factors for developing AKI. His past medical history (type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, ischaemic heart disease, CKD, anaemia) are all associated with increased AKI risk. Prior to surgery anaemia could be corrected but the remainder would be difficult to modify in a timely fashion prior to urgent surgery. On admission to ICU there is evidence of worsening anaemia, hypovolaemia, and hyperglycaemia. All of these could have been avoided during surgery and can be managed on ICU and therefore are modifiable risks. Options B–D include non-modifiable factors like diabetes, malignancy, and ischaemic heart disease.
questions.topic: AKI modifiable risk factors perioperative hypovolaemia anaemia hyperglycaemia
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, hypertension and chronic kidney disease has been admitted to the intensive care unit after open surgery for an abdominal aortic aneurysm. His actual weight is estimated at 130 kg. His baseline creatinine is 278 µmol/L and the following day he has a serum creatinine of 341 µmol/L. His urine output is between 0.5 to 1 mls/kg/hr. The following is true about his renal function.
options: A: He has an AKI based on his urine output alone.
B: He has chronic kidney disease and therefore his AKI status cannot be determined.
C: He has an AKI stage 2 based on his serum creatinine.
D: He has an AKI stage 1 based on his serum creatinine.
E: He has not yet reached the threshold for the diagnosis of AKI based on serum creatinine and his is exhibiting appropriate post-surgical oliguria.
questions.explanation: The increase in serum creatinine exceeds the 26.5 µmol/L within 48 hours required to define AKI stage 1 according to KDIGO criteria (278→341 = +63 µmol/L). The purpose of this criterion is to prevent patients with high baseline creatinine from being missed. If baseline SCr is 60, a x1.5 increase to 90 won't take long, but if baseline SCr is 278, a x1.5 increase to 417 is going to take time. The rapid increase ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48h was introduced to help recognise AKI in such patients. The fact that the increase exceeds 26.5 does not mean it is a greater stage of AKI. Urine output is 0.5–1 ml/kg/hr which is normal.
questions.topic: KDIGO AKI staging high baseline creatinine CKD
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: An 82 year old woman has been admitted to intensive care with biliary sepsis. She has been in intensive care for 24 hours. Although she is frail with a weight of 48 kg, she has minimal past medical history and no known chronic kidney disease and no further information about her is available. She has been given 4.5g piperacillin-tazobactam and has been fluid resuscitated. She is requiring norepinephrine at 0.1 µg/kg/min to maintain a mean arterial pressure of > 65 mmHg. Her urine output over the last 12 hours has averaged 20 mL/hour and her serum creatinine is 88 88 µmol l/L. The following is true regarding her renal function.
options: A: She has oliguria consistent with AKI but her serum creatinine of 88 µmol/L is reassuring that AKI has not yet developed.
B: It is not possible to comment on her renal function because her baseline serum creatinine is unknown.
C: Her renal function is normal with an appropriate decrease in her urine output and a normal serum creatinine.
D: She has AKI stage 3 based on urine output
E: She has AKI stage 2 based on her urine output over 12 hours.
questions.explanation: The urine output is low and consistent with AKI. UO = 20 mL/h for a 48 kg patient = 0.42 ml/kg/h, which is <0.5 ml/kg/h for ≥12 hours, meeting KDIGO Stage 2 urine output criterion. Although the serum creatinine is only 88 µmol/L, the woman's fragility and low weight suggest, in the absence of a past medical history of chronic kidney disease, that her baseline creatinine is likely to be low and an increase of x1.5 or of >26.5 µmol/L is certainly possible, if not likely. A low creatinine does not exclude AKI in patients with low muscle mass.
questions.topic: AKI diagnosis urine output frail elderly low muscle mass creatinine limitations
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Antibiotics associated directly with an increased risk of acute kidney injury include:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Penicillins

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Fluoroquinolones

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Aminoglycosides

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Macrolides

questions.explanation: Penicillins, fluoroquinolones, and aminoglycosides are all directly associated with nephrotoxicity. Macrolides are not directly associated with nephrotoxicity and in some cases may be protective. However, macrolides are potent cytochrome P450 inhibitors and therefore may be associated with kidney injury indirectly through interactions with other drugs.
questions.topic: AKI nephrotoxic antibiotics aminoglycosides fluoroquinolones penicillins macrolides
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Recovery from sepsis related AKI or AKD

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Recovery can be assumed to be a full recovery if the serum creatinine returns to normal

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Full recovery is observed in 95% of patients admitted to the intensive care with sepsis related AKD

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Is associated with an increased risk of mortality even if renal recovery is complete

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Recovery from AKI or AKD is more likely in patients with underlying CKD stage 1

questions.explanation: Serum creatinine is a poor measure of renal function in AKI. Either SCr may be slow to recover despite improving renal function, or SCr may be falsely low, especially in longer term ICU patients in whom muscle wasting has occurred. Approximately 50% of patients with sepsis related AKD will not have renal function return to baseline by the time they leave hospital. Patients who have an AKI which recovers have a higher mortality than patients with no AKI. Patients with any severity of underlying CKD are more likely to progress to more severe CKD or end stage renal failure if they suffer AKI.
questions.topic: AKI AKD sepsis recovery creatinine limitations CKD progression mortality
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The following statements are true regarding disease related acute kidney injury

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Urine output criteria for AKI is the preferred measurement for AKI associated with major surgery

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Radio-opaque contrast media should be avoided in patients with trauma related AKI

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. A transfusion target of a haemoglobin of 90 g/L may avoid the risk of AKI associated.

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Sepsis related AKI has a better renal prognosis than non-sepsis related AKI

questions.explanation: The incidence of AKI sharply increases if urine output rather than serum creatinine criteria are used post major surgery. It is unclear which criteria are best for assessing peri-operative renal function since both methods are flawed. The impact of contrast media on the progression of AKI is probably overstated in most situations — the exception may be cardiac procedures where large volumes are used, but in most other cases the benefits of a proper diagnostic scan outweigh the risks to kidney function. Anaemia is an independent risk factor for AKI but there is no evidence to support a liberal transfusion target in most patients in order to avoid AKI. Outcome from sepsis related AKI is poorer than non-sepsis related AKI.
questions.topic: AKI surgery contrast media anaemia transfusion sepsis prognosis
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: When assessing renal recovery from AKI or AKD, the following statements are true

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. A raised serum creatinine above baseline indicates persistence of AKI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Measurement of the fractional excretions of sodium and urea may be useful in determining the persistence of AKD.

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Measured creatinine clearance may be useful in determining renal recovery, particularly in patients with prolonged ICU admission.

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Recovery is complete when the patient's serum creatinine returns to normal.

questions.explanation: Although serum creatinine remains the most convenient biomarker of renal function its interpretation can be very problematic. In defining AKI and recovery a patient's baseline serum creatinine is required. If unknown, baseline may not be available. 'Normal' serum creatinine has a wide range depending on gender, body habitus, age and ethnicity. Prolonged ICU admission is associated with decreased muscle mass and an associated decline in serum creatinine — SCr does not follow renal function reliably in the acute clinical setting. Fractional excretions of sodium and urea have not proved useful in defining renal recovery. Urine collection and measurement of creatinine clearance may be useful — 4 or 8-hour urine collections are probably the optimum for clinical use outside of research.
questions.topic: AKI AKD recovery assessment creatinine clearance FENa sarcopenia
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The following may be considered modifiable risk factors for developing an acute kidney injury:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Ethnicity

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Male sex

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Ischaemic Heart Disease

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Hypovolaemia

questions.explanation: Non-modifiable risk factors for developing AKI include older age, Afro-American descent and certain chronic medical conditions that increase the risk of developing AKI. Other conditions include hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cardiac failure, ischaemic heart disease, chronic kidney disease, obesity and some forms of malignancy. The only modifiable risk listed above for AKI is hypovolaemia.
questions.topic: AKI modifiable non-modifiable risk factors hypovolaemia
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Certain co-morbidities are associated with an increased risk of developing AKI in hospital. These include:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Heart failure

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Obesity

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Hypertension

questions.explanation: 20% of patients admitted to hospital with heart failure will develop an AKI and the development of AKI is associated with an increased risk of mortality. COPD is an independent risk factor for the development of both CKD and AKI. Obesity may increase the risk of AKI through changes to venous blood flow and pressures and through systemic inflammation. Hypertension is an independent risk factor for AKI.
questions.topic: AKI comorbidities heart failure COPD obesity hypertension risk factors
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: A 72-year-old man has had an elective knee replacement. His has a significant past medical history of ischaemic heart disease, type-2 diabetes mellitus, chronic renal disease (CKD stage 3) and sleep apnoea secondary to obesity. His pre-operative serum creatinine was 172 µmol/L. His serum creatinine has increased to 220 µmol/L but he is in considerable pain and you have been asked to review his drug chart. His current medications include; ramipril, bisoprolol, furosemide, atorvastatin and doxazocin (usual medications) and ibuprofen, oral morphine, ondansetron, omeprazole and dalteparin (started since surgery). The following would be the best course of action.
options: A: Modifications to the drug chart are unnecessary at this point, because he has not yet developed severe acute kidney injury.
B: Stop all of his usual medications, stop the ibuprofen, omeprazole and oral morphine. Start oral oxycodone and change the dalteparin to unfractionated heparin. Continue the ondansetron.

C: Continue ramipril, bisoprolol, furosemide and doxazocin because these are his normal medications. Stop the ibuprofen and omeprazole since these are nephrotoxins and change the dalteparin to unfractionated heparin.

D: Continue the bisoprolol, stop ramipril and furosemide and review need for doxazocin. Stop the ibuprofen, change the morphine to oral oxycodone, continue the omeprazole and continue the dalteparin but with anti-Xa monitoring.

E: Stop the ramipril, continue the bisoprolol furosemide and doxazocin. Stop the ibuprofen and omeprazole but continue with the morphine and ondansetron. Continue the dalteparin with anti-Xa monitoring.
questions.explanation: Reviews of drug charts can be complicated when considering acute problems on the background of chronic disease. Bisoprolol should be continued if possible because of the risk of tachyarrhythmias secondary to acute withdrawal. Ramipril should be temporarily stopped because of its potential impact on AKI although it had an overall protective effect in many cases of CKD. Furosemide should be held until the patient's fluid status is optimised peri-operatively — furosemide does not protect the kidneys from AKI. Ibuprofen is a potent nephrotoxin and should be avoided in the peri-operative patient with additional risks of AKI (e.g. diabetes mellitus, CKD). Omeprazole is a potential nephrotoxin although most nephrotoxicity is observed after long term use — its advantages protecting against stress ulcers outweigh the risk of AKI in most major peri-operative patients. Morphine has a potent renally excreted metabolite which may accumulate in renal impairment. Oxycodone is less effected by kidney injury. Low molecular weight heparins may accumulate in AKI but they can be safely used if anti-Xa measurements are used to check for accumulation.
questions.topic: AKI drug review nephrotoxins NSAIDs ACE-inhibitors morphine LMWH perioperative
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The following may be considered non-modifiable risk factors for developing an acute kidney injury

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Female sex

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Increasing age

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Afro-American descent

questions.explanation: Two recent meta-analyses including one with over 6 million patients found that men are more likely to develop HA-AKI with an odds ratio of 1.23 [1.11, 1.36] (Neugarten 2018, Grams 2015). Older age, Afro-American descent and certain chronic medical conditions increase the risk of developing AKI. COPD is a non-modifiable chronic condition associated with increased AKI risk.
questions.topic: AKI non-modifiable risk factors age sex ethnicity COPD
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Concerning AKI after major surgery the following statements are true:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Major surgery is a risk factor for AKI

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Approximately 40% of patients develop at least stage 1 AKI after major surgery

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Urine output is more reliable for diagnosing AKI after surgery compared to serum creatinine

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. The definition of stage 1 AKI is different after major surgery because of the dilutional effects of administered fluids affecting the serum creatinine.

questions.explanation: Major surgery is a risk factor for AKI. A recent prospective study in 30 countries in more than 10,000 patients undergoing major surgery showed that approximately one in five (20%) of patients developed AKI after major surgery. The diagnosis of AKI immediately after surgery can be problematic because of dilutional effects of fluids on serum creatinine and physiological changes in urine output caused by increased ADH secretion after surgery, but definitions for AKI have not been changed to accommodate this. There is no evidence that urine output measurements are better than serum creatinine measurements at diagnosing AKI in this group of patients.
questions.topic: AKI major surgery incidence diagnosis creatinine urine output perioperative
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Regarding the recovery from AKI

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. AKI recovery may be under-estimated by changes to serum creatinine caused by sarcopenia

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Unknown baseline serum creatine is a known confounder in the assessment of recovery from AKI

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Fractional excretion of sodium or natriuresis are useful measurements for predicting renal recovery

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Several hours of delay are expected before observing a decrease in serum creatinine after full normalization of glomerular filtration rate after AKI

questions.explanation: Sarcopenia is a cause of over-estimation of renal recovery as serum creatinines decrease in response to decreased synthesis (not under-estimation). Unknown baseline serum creatinine confounds both the definitions of AKI and the assessment of its recovery. Fraction excretion of sodium has been used to predict renal recovery in the past, but evidence suggests it is not useful in the short term. GFR may improve before the serum creatinine decreases.
questions.topic: AKI recovery sarcopenia creatinine baseline GFR FENa
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Modifiable risk factors for developing AKI include:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. High volume fluid resuscitation

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. The administration of furosemide

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Protein pump inhibitors

questions.explanation: Excessive fluid resuscitation, particularly in inflammatory conditions, is likely to cause tissue oedema which includes the renal parenchyma, increasing intracapsular pressures and decreasing renal blood flow. Furosemide is not considered nephrotoxic although injudicious use of diuretics may render a patient hypovolaemic thereby risking the development of AKI. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs have a well-recognised association with AKI and should be avoided in the management of most critical illness and peri-operative pain management of patients with other risk factors for AKI. Protein pump inhibitors are associated with AKI, particularly if combined with other medications such as fluoroquinolones and penicillins (Ikuta K, et al. BMJ Open. 2021).
questions.topic: AKI modifiable risk factors fluid overload NSAIDs PPI furosemide
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: Acute kidney disease (AKD)

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Is defined as stage 2 or 3 AKI persisting for more than 14 days

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. Is defined as any stage of AKI persisting for more than 7 days

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Is redefined as chronic kidney disease (CKD) if it persists for more than 90 days

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Is redefined as CKD if stage 2 or 3 persists for more than 90 days

questions.explanation: AKD occurs in any patient in whom an AKI persists for more than 7 days and less than 90 days. It is not dependent on the stage of AKI observed nor on the severity of the renal impairment ongoing. After 90 days, ongoing renal impairment is considered chronic and staged accordingly as CKD.
questions.topic: AKD definition AKI duration CKD 7 days 90 days
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: The following are true of AKI developing as a consequence of critical illness:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A. Approximately 40% of patients with AKI stage 2 and 3 will progress to AKD

statements.label: B
statements.text: B. If AKD does not resolve by hospital discharge, it is associated with a significantly increased mortality at 1 year

statements.label: C
statements.text: C. Patients who recover renal function rapidly within 24-48 hours of developing AKI have a similar mortality risk at 1 years as patients without AKI

statements.label: D
statements.text: D. Patients who develop AKD are at increased risk of developing CKD

questions.explanation: All AKI is associated with poorer outcome, regardless of whether or not it recovers quickly. However early recovery and less severe AKI are associated with lower risk of morbidity (including CKD) and mortality than patients who develop severe AKI or who progress to AKD. Even patients who recover rapidly within 24-48 hours still have increased mortality risk compared to patients without AKI.
questions.topic: AKI critical illness AKD progression CKD mortality early recovery
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: A 42-year-old man has been in intensive care for 108 days after contracting COVID. He has had a tracheostomy and is slowly weaning from the ventilator. He required renal replacement therapy 10 days after admission, although he is now off haemodiafiltration, passing good volumes of urine. His baseline serum creatinine was 95 µmol/L and his current serum creatinine is 178 µmol/L. His serum creatinine peaked at 398 µmol/L prior to renal replacement therapy. The following statement is true regarding his renal function.
options: A: He is now classed as having acute kidney disease, with an increased risk of developing chronic kidney disease.
B: His acute kidney injury is ongoing with an increased risk of developing acute kidney disease and later chronic kidney disease.
C: He has stage 1 acute kidney injury.
D: He is now classed as having chronic kidney disease.
E: Although he has developed acute kidney disease, he has a good chance of return to normal renal function.
questions.explanation: The definition of acute kidney disease is the criteria for AKI persisting for more than 7 days but less than 90 days. After 90 days, ongoing renal impairment is considered chronic and staged accordingly. This man developed AKI 10 days after admission and still has renal impairment 98 days later (108 - 10 = 98 days, which exceeds 90 days). He is now classed as having chronic kidney disease.
questions.topic: AKD CKD definition 90 days COVID prolonged ICU
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology

questions.question: A 56-year-old woman has been on the ICU for 53 days. She was admitted with a complex abdominal infection and has suffered recurrent bouts of sepsis caused by recurrent and persistent intra-abdominal abscesses. Prior to admission she was fit and will with normal renal function. Her baseline serum creatinine was 68 µmol/L. Early during her admission, she became anuric and her serum creatine increased to 320 µmol/L requiring renal replacement therapy. Now her condition is better, her sepsis appears to be under control, and she no longer requires renal replacement therapy. She is passing > 0.5 mL/kg/h of urine, and her serum creatinine is 88 µmol/L. The following can be said to be true.
options: A: She developed stage 3 AKI but now her renal function has fully recovered. With this degree of recovery, she only has a very small risk of developing AKI or CKD in the future.

B: She developed stage 3 AKI and renal function has improved but scale of improvement is difficult to determine because of the long-term impact of critical illness of serum creatinine measurements. She remains at risk of further AKI or CKD.
C: It is impossible to state if renal function has improved because of the impact of critical illness of serum creatinine measurements. She remains at risk of further AKI or CKD.
D: She developed stage 3 AKI but now her renal function has fully recovered although she remains at risk of further AKI and CKD in the future.
questions.explanation: Patients with prolonged critical illness become increasingly difficult to assess for AKI based on their serum creatinine. Marked sarcopenia associated with critical illness makes assessment of renal recovery difficult. Measurement of creatinine clearance over 4–8 hours or over 24 hours if the patient is very stable may be required for assessment of renal recovery. Her creatinine of 88 (above baseline 68) could reflect incomplete recovery or simply reduced creatinine production from muscle wasting. She remains at risk of further AKI or CKD.
questions.topic: AKI recovery assessment sarcopenia creatinine clearance prolonged ICU CKD risk
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part II: Epidemiology
